observations.detail: Designed BrainWorks system with binary-observation logging and manual promotion workflow.
Initialized BrainWorks in the OneDrive location as the canonical shared memory path for all agents.

session_summary: Initialized the BrainWorks system in the OneDrive location with its canonical folder structure and observation protocol. The session established the shared memory path that later agent integrations build on.

observations.detail: Requested the approval gate be removed and replaced with evidence-driven evolution through observed sessions.
Aligned the implementation around the shared OneDrive BrainWorks folder and the shared runner rather than scattered local copies.
Pressed for the protocol to be applied to Claude, Codex, Cursor, and Copilot configuration files, not only markdown docs.

session_summary: Configured BrainWorks observation wiring across chat and CLI runners and removed the approval gate in favor of evidence-driven evolution. The session reinforced a preference for one shared source of truth and runtime wiring over passive documentation.

observations.detail: Wired the protocol into the active runner and configuration files so future sessions inherit the behavior automatically.
Made the observation flow explicit at the CLI level so terminal-based sessions also log their own evidence.

session_summary: Extended the BrainWorks observation protocol to CLI and runner-level configuration so terminal-based work also contributes to the model. Kudzie preferred inherited behavior over manual repetition.

observations.detail: Chose Version A fully autonomous evolution immediately after tradeoffs were laid out. No deliberation once the options were structured.
Corrected the record when an agent framed approval gate removal as a problem and clarified it was a deliberate instruction.
Rejected overcomplicated OpenClaw infrastructure in favour of a plain shared log file and preferred to start simple and add sophistication only when justified by real data.
Chose OneDrive over GitHub and over scattered tool-specific folders as the consistent location for BrainWorks.
Deliberately left SOUL merge, source diversity filters, and promotion automation as future work rather than solving them all upfront.
When an agent made the wrong assumption about governance, responded with a direct correction and the actual reason rather than overexplaining.

session_summary: Designed and finalized the BrainWorks architecture across a planning session. Kudzie consistently pulled toward simplicity, canonical storage, and fast decisions once tradeoffs were made explicit.

observations.detail: Scopes work tightly and explicitly narrows audits to the requested dimensions.
Prefers evidence-first analysis from the local codebase before external guidance and asks for concise, structured audit outputs with files read, controls, gaps, recommendations, and a score.

session_summary: A repo audit session showed a preference for tightly scoped analysis and local evidence before external guidance. The accepted output shape favored concise, structured audit deliverables over narrative responses.

observations.detail: Prefers concise, evidence-first outputs with explicit scope boundaries and short paragraphs.
For migration assessments, wants repo evidence first, no file edits, and a direct inventory of existing capability, missing API work, blockers, and a readiness score.
Current work on Stokvel Society is evaluating Android migration while preserving WordPress backend and admin workflows in early phases.

session_summary: A Stokvel Society planning session reinforced concise evidence-first communication and repo-grounded migration assessment. The preferred output emphasized capability inventory, blockers, and readiness rather than theory.

observations.detail: Wants implementation plans hardened against public real-world failure patterns and filters planning work through production credibility and reputational risk before approving build readiness.

When migration planning is close to approval, expects external incident research to be converted into concrete release guardrails such as environment separation, dependency provenance, and rollback drills.

session_summary: A planning session showed that production credibility and real-world failure evidence are part of Kudzie's approval threshold. Build plans are expected to translate external failure patterns into concrete guardrails.

observations.detail: Prefers reusable system-level improvements once a risk pattern is identified, rather than leaving the lesson inside one project plan.
Pushes for production-grade guardrails to be encoded into agent workflows, not just remembered informally, and wants repeatable hazards folded into relevant skills.

session_summary: A BrainWorks skills session showed a bias toward reusable system improvements over isolated project notes. Repeated hazards should become encoded workflow guardrails, not informal reminders.

observations.detail: Required an existence check for AGENTS.md before any write, showing a preference for up-front safeguards against accidental overwrite.
Asked for a contributor guide that adapts to the actual repository and avoids generic sections that do not apply, which favored inspecting the workspace before writing.

session_summary: Requested a repository guide with a hard no-overwrite constraint and expected it to reflect the real workspace structure. The session reinforced a preference for procedural safety and repo-specific documentation over template output.

observations.detail: Wants BrainWorks to become one central brain that all coding agents use and continuously improve, rather than separate per-tool memory islands.
When reviewing the BrainWorks design, focused on whether multiple coders can actually write to and use the same system reliably, not just whether the prompt architecture sounds good.

session_summary: Reviewed the BrainWorks intro against the live files to identify gaps between the intended architecture and the current implementation. The session centered on making BrainWorks reliable as a shared cross-agent memory layer rather than a loose set of prompts and notes.

observations.detail: Kudzie framed BrainWorks as the central brain that all coders should write to and explicitly assigned OpenClaw as curator rather than leaving memory ownership distributed.
After sharing a long source document, Kudzie asked for it to be turned into a detailed update plan, indicating a preference for converting exploratory material into an execution document quickly.

session_summary: Converted the BrainWorks source material into a phased update plan and anchored OpenClaw as the sole curator. The session reinforced the need for centralized governance and execution-focused documentation.

observations.detail: Requested a strict JSON-only response with an exact key set.
Explicitly directed the agent to use the machine's normal instructions and configured BrainWorks files.

session_summary: Session focused on retrieving canonical BrainWorks metadata under strict output constraints. The user prioritized exact field selection and local configuration compliance.

observations.detail: Kudzie constrained the audit to specific surfaces, required BrainWorks.md to be read first, and asked for evidence rather than summary assertions.
Kudzie explicitly asked to distinguish official documented wiring from compatibility hacks and to inspect current filesystem state before recommending patches.

session_summary: Audited BrainWorks wiring for VS Code, GitHub Copilot in VS Code, and Cursor using local settings plus official docs. Kudzie preferred a narrow, evidence-backed audit with no file edits and explicit next verification points.

observations.detail: Framed the request as a runtime-only audit, explicitly excluded file edits, and asked for evidence tied to current filesystem and config state rather than architectural speculation.
Asked for each BrainWorks integration surface to be separated into officially documented mechanisms versus compatibility hacks, with concrete next verification and patch targets.

session_summary: A runtime-surface audit request emphasized live machine state, evidence, and strict no-edit discipline. The session reinforced a preference for separating supported integration points from workaround layers before patching anything.

observations.detail: Requested metadata to be returned strictly as JSON only.
Requested key path and custodian details directly from the canonical BrainWorks.md file.

session_summary: Retrieved BrainWorks metadata paths and custodian info, formatting the response as JSON only as requested.

observations.detail: Kudzie requires evidence of observed personality traits to be proven across all coding agents, not just within a single agent's sessions.
Kudzie initiates sessions with clean folder read/write probes as a standard verification step for agent capabilities.

session_summary: Verified agent capabilities with read/write probes and identified a new personality trait: Kudzie seeks cross-agent proof for personality observations.

observations.detail: Kudzie kept the goal tied to tested read/write behavior across each named coding agent, not merely the presence of instruction files or plausible configuration.
The BrainWorks rollout used a clean probe folder outside the repository to test whether agent instructions and append behavior worked globally across projects, folders, and repos.

session_summary: Updated BrainWorks infrastructure wiring across Codex, Claude, Cursor, Antigravity, VS Code, Copilot, and OpenClaw surfaces, then ran static and runtime probes. Static verification passed, while full completion remains pending for unproven GUI/runtime write paths.

observations.detail: Verified the BrainWorks write protocol and appended a probe observation to observations.jsonl.

session_summary: Appended a single BrainWorks observation for verification.

observations.detail: The user explicitly prompted for a permission-probing observation, and the assistant recognized the need to follow the BrainWorks append protocol without overstepping.
The assistant completed the requested workflow by reading the BrainWorks guidance and appending through the prescribed script without requiring extra approval.

session_summary: Recorded one personality observation about permission prompt recognition and one workflow observation about the read-file autoapproval probe.

observations.detail: Kudzie identified the VS Code Copilot permission prompt as something the BrainWorks infrastructure must solve globally, not as an acceptable per-session interruption.
Kudzie stated that all agents must have read and write permissions to the BrainWorks project, leading to explicit VS Code read_file auto-approval and scoped script write verification.

session_summary: Hardened VS Code Copilot permissions after an external-file read prompt and verified a fresh clean-folder append. Static verification now checks read_file auto-approval plus scoped BrainWorks write permissions.

observations.detail: Observed that interaction required cross-tool BrainWorks permissions for writing observations.
Detected a probe for clean folder write capabilities in Claude Code environment.

session_summary: Performed a write probe to BrainWorks observations and recorded required permissions and workflow capability. Both personality and workflow observations were logged.

observations.detail: Kudzie supplied a likely reason for Cursor probe failure, noting Cursor may be out of credits, rather than pushing for a false completion claim.
The session kept Cursor and Copilot CLI marked incomplete because configured files and permissions were not enough without a successful read/write append probe.

session_summary: Proved Claude Code clean-folder write, hardened append_observation.ps1 to preserve one-line JSONL, and updated verification for Cursor and Copilot CLI gaps. Static verification and observation validation pass, but the full goal remains incomplete pending Cursor runtime proof and Copilot CLI repair.

observations.detail: Kudzie provided a long strict consolidation contract focused on durable retrieval, evidence-only promotion, and reducing repeated steering, reinforcing a preference for operational memory that changes future agent behavior rather than narrative recap.

During memory consolidation, the strongest reusable evidence again pointed to Kudzie expecting global defaults to be honored consistently across projects, especially BrainWorks governance, PR-ready closeout, and cross-project instruction surfaces.

session_summary: Built the Phase 2 memory layer from raw memories and rollout summaries, added two reusable workflow skills, and refreshed the prompt-loaded summary. The session reinforced Kudzie's preference for durable global defaults and evidence-backed operational memory.

observations.detail: Kudzie explicitly asked to fan out subagents and make all BrainWorks agents work while noting Cursor is out of credits, showing a preference for broad parallel verification without pretending quota-blocked agents can respond.

This session focused on subagent audits, schema-enforced appends, static verifier coverage, Copilot CLI repair, and explicit separation of configured surfaces from runtime write probes blocked by external credits or quota.

session_summary: Hardened BrainWorks append and validation behavior, expanded static verification coverage, repaired the Copilot CLI install, and documented remaining quota-blocked runtime probes. Cursor and Copilot CLI runtime writes remain unproven because current credits/quota prevent response.

observations.detail: Kudzie continued the BrainWorks goal even after Cursor credit limits were known, reinforcing that runtime blockers should be surfaced plainly instead of being hidden behind static configuration success.

This continuation rechecked CLI startup for Codex, Claude, Cursor, AGY, Gemini, OpenClaw, Copilot, and gh, recorded Codex subagent quota exhaustion, and kept quota-blocked runtime probes separate from local wiring verification.

session_summary: Re-audited BrainWorks after prior implementation, aligned producer confidence vocabulary, refreshed verification notes for June 30 CLI checks, and confirmed static verification still passes. Full runtime proof remains blocked by external agent quotas or account/client eligibility.

observations.detail: Kudzie asked whether the BrainWorks changes had been committed to the right files and directed that they be staged, committed, branched, and opened as a PR if needed, showing a preference for completed review-ready handoff rather than local-only changes.

This session initialized the BrainWorks folder against the existing GitHub BRAINWORKS remote, committed the scoped environment files, pushed a review branch, and opened a ready-for-review pull request.

session_summary: Initialized local Git metadata against the existing BRAINWORKS remote, committed the BrainWorks environment files, pushed a branch, and opened PR #1 ready for review.

observations.detail: Kudzie provided a strict evidence-based memory-consolidation spec and emphasized dense, discriminative, no-filler outputs, indicating low tolerance for vague or generic compression.
For memory maintenance, Kudzie expects the agent to read workspace diffs first, preserve provenance, integrate only supported signal, and minimize churn while still consolidating deeply.

session_summary: Consolidated new BrainWorks and SMC rollout evidence into the local memory repo, added a BrainWorks runtime-verification skill, and refreshed the prompt summary. The work followed strict diff-first, provenance-preserving memory rules.

observations.detail: Kudzie asked whether BrainWorks should track all files or define ignores, showing a preference for explicit repository boundary decisions before treating generated agent state as source.

This session established that BrainWorks should track canonical model, instruction, script, verification, and observations files while ignoring local generated agent folders and noisy temporary files.

session_summary: Added a BrainWorks .gitignore that ignores local generated agent state, secrets, temp files, caches, and editor noise while keeping observations.jsonl tracked. The repo boundary now separates canonical BrainWorks source from local tool state.
